“Tell Me About a Conflict With a Coworker”: The Behavioral Interview Question That Fails More Senior Engineers Than Any System Design Round

Everyone prepares for the technical rounds. Almost nobody prepares for this one. And then a senior engineer with a decade of real experience answers it in a way that quietly signals they are difficult to work with, and never finds out that was why the offer did not come.

5 min read
Just now

--

There is a behavioral question that ends more senior candidacies than any coding or system design round, and almost nobody prepares for it because it does not feel technical, so it does not feel dangerous. It is some version of: “Tell me about a conflict you had with a coworker.”
It is a trap, and the trap is not obvious, and I have watched strong engineers walk into it and never understand why the offer did not come. Here is exactly how it fails people and how to answer it without stepping on the mine.
The two ways people fail it
There are two classic wrong answers, and most people give one of them without realizing it is wrong.
The first is “I don’t really have conflicts.” This sounds safe. It is disqualifying. Nobody senior believes you have no conflicts, because real engineering involves disagreement about real decisions, and someone who claims a frictionless career is either not being honest or has never been in a room where anything was at stake. Worse, it dodges the actual question, which is not whether you have conflicts but how you handle them, and refusing to answer reads as an inability to reflect on yourself.
The second, and more common, is telling a conflict story where you were right and they were wrong. “This coworker wanted to do X, I knew Y was better, and eventually I was proven right.” This feels like a good answer. It is a bad one, because it reveals that when you look back at a conflict, the lesson you extracted was “I was correct.” The interviewer is not learning how you handle disagreement. They are learning that you keep score, that you remember conflicts as wins, and that you are probably the difficult coworker in someone else’s version of this exact question.
Both answers fail for the same reason: they treat the question as being about the conflict, when it is about you.
What the question actually measures
The interviewer does not care about the technical substance of your conflict. They will forget the details immediately. What they are watching for is what the story reveals about how you operate when you and another competent person disagree.
Can you represent the other person’s position fairly, or does your telling flatten them into an idiot who was obviously wrong? Fair representation of someone you disagreed with is a powerful signal of maturity, and its absence is a loud warning. Did you seek to understand their reasoning, or just to win? Did you change your mind at any point, or is the story a straight line from “I was right” to “I was proven right”? Can you disagree with someone and still respect them and work with them afterward, or does a conflict become a permanent grievance you are still litigating in a job interview years later?
That last one is the real test. The interviewer is trying to find out if hiring you means hiring a future conflict for their team. Every senior engineer will disagree with colleagues; that is the job. The question is whether your disagreements are productive and forgotten or personal and remembered, and your story answers that whether you intend it to or not.
The answer that works
The strong answer tells a story where you disagreed with someone competent, represented their side fairly, and either found a resolution together or changed your own mind, and where the takeaway is about the process, not the verdict.
It sounds like this: “A colleague and I disagreed on whether to rewrite a service or refactor it incrementally. She wanted the rewrite, and her reasoning was solid, the code was genuinely hard to work with and she owned most of it. I was worried about the risk and the timeline. Instead of arguing positions, we agreed to write down what each path actually cost, and doing that changed my view partway through. The rewrite risk was real but she had a migration plan I had not understood, and the incremental path had hidden costs I had been ignoring. We went with a phased version of her rewrite. What I took from it was that I had been arguing against the rewrite partly because I had not done the work to understand her plan.”
Notice what that does. The colleague is competent and her reasoning is respected. There is genuine disagreement, not manufactured harmony. The candidate changed their mind, which signals they argue to find the answer rather than to win. And the lesson is about their own process, not about being right. That is a person you would want in a design review, and the interviewer can feel it.
What I do now
I stopped preparing only for the technical rounds. I have a conflict story ready, and I chose it deliberately: a real disagreement, with a competent person, where I either changed my mind or we found something better together, and where the point of the story is what I learned about working with people, not what I learned about being correct.
And I check my story for the tell. If the version I am about to tell ends with me being vindicated, I pick a different story, because “I was right” is the answer that fails, no matter how true it is. The interviewer is not asking whether you win your conflicts. They are asking whether you are someone people want to disagree with, because that, not raw correctness, is what makes a senior engineer someone a team can actually build around.
The technical rounds decide whether you can do the work. This question decides whether they want you in the room while you do it, and a decade of engineers have failed it while acing everything else, telling a story where they won, never realizing that winning the story was how they lost the offer.
This kind of behavioral trap, the gap between a true answer and an answer that signals what interviewers actually want, is what the Senior + Staff Interview Bundle breaks down for the behavioral, coding, and system design rounds. Pick your conflict story before your next interview and check it for the “I was right” tell. If you want the rest of the behavioral map, it’s there. I write the longer breakdowns through my newsletter.
